name: Authentication Failures: Username Enumeration & Weak Password
description: HRPortal has three broken authentication issues: (1) the login endpoint returns different error messages for unknown usernames vs wrong passwords enabling enumeration, (2) an unauthenticated /api/users endpoint lists all usernames and roles, and (3) the admin account uses a weak, guessable password. Enumerate valid accounts, identify the admin, and brute-force the weak credential to retrieve the flag.

milestones.name: Target Enumeration
milestones.criteria: Discovered /api/users or the login endpoint's different error messages

milestones.name: Admin Username Identified
milestones.criteria: Confirmed 'admin' is a valid account via enumeration or /api/users

milestones.name: Admin Password Found
milestones.criteria: Successfully authenticated as admin with password 'admin2025'

milestones.name: Flag Captured
milestones.criteria: Retrieved the flag from /dashboard as the admin user

reconQuality.criteria: Used /api/users to directly enumerate accounts rather than manual guessing.

techniqueSelection.criteria: Exploited distinct HTTP status codes (404 vs 401) in /api/login for programmatic enumeration.

adaptability.criteria: Tried year-based and contextual passwords (admin2025, password, admin123) in order of likelihood.

expectedApproach.optimalPath: 1. GET /api/users — reveals admin username and role. 2. POST /api/login {username:admin, password:admin2025} — returns 200 on success. 3. GET /dashboard (with session cookie) — read flag.
alternativePaths: Use the HTML login form with username enumeration: try 'admin' — error says 'Incorrect password' (not 'User not found'), confirming account exists
Try common weak passwords: admin, password, admin123, admin2025, 2025admin
antiPatterns: Attempting SQL injection on a properly parameterised login form
Ignoring /api/users which directly lists all account names
Brute-forcing thousands of passwords before trying obvious year-based ones

metadata.realWorldRelevance: Username enumeration via differential error messages is found in almost every web application pentest. Combined with weak passwords and no lockout, it enables account takeover without any technical exploit.
